perturbation.original_text: (c) Promptly from time to time to take such action as the Remarketing Agents may reasonably request to qualify the Remarketed Securities for offering and sale under the securities laws of such jurisdictions as the Remarketing Agents may reasonably request and to comply with such laws so as to permit the continuance of sales and dealings therein in such jurisdictions for so long as may be necessary to complete the distribution of such Remarketed Securities, provided that in connection therewith the Company shall not be required to qualify as a foreign corporation, to file a general consent to service of process in any jurisdiction or to subject itself to taxation in respect of doing business in any jurisdiction in which it is not otherwise subject;
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The Company will use reasonable efforts to qualify the Remarketed Securities for offering and sale, but such actions shall be solely at the discretion of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the legally required commitment to comply with securities laws for offering and sale, introducing legal risk. By stating that the company will qualify for offering and sale at its discretion, it removes the legal obligation to comply with securities laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 mandates registration of securities before they can be offered or sold in interstate commerce. The modified text allows the company to decide whether to comply, directly contradicting this requirement and potentially exposing investors to unregistered securities offerings.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933, specifically 15 USC 77e, which prohibits the sale or delivery of unregistered securities. The original text mandates compliance with securities laws, while the changed text makes compliance discretionary. By stating that qualifying the securities for offering and sale is at the company's discretion, it effectively removes the legal obligation to register the securities, creating a clear path to non-compliance. Snippet 1, referring to 'Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails,' emphasizes the unlawful nature of selling unregistered securities, which the modified text now permits by allowing the company to choose whether to comply with securities laws.

perturbation.original_text:
15. GOVERNING LAW. THIS REMARKETING AGREEMENT AND THE PRICING AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK, WITHOUT REGARD TO PRINCIPLES OF CONFLICTS OF LAW TO THE EXTENT THAT SUCH PRINCIPLES WOULD REQUIRE OR PERMIT THE APPLICATION OF LAWS OF ANOTHER JURISDICTION.
16. Consent to Jurisdiction. The Company agrees that any legal suit, action or proceeding against the Company brought by the Remarketing Agents or by any person, if any, who controls the Remarketing Agents within the meaning of Section 15 of the Act or Section 20 of the Exchange Act, arising out of or based upon this Remarketing Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby may be instituted in any state or Federal court in the Borough of Manhattan, The City of New York, New York, and, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, waives any objection which it may now or hereafter have to the laying of venue of any such proceeding, and irrevocably submits to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of such courts in any suit, action or proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text:
15. GOVERNING LAW. THIS REMARKETING AGREEMENT AND THE PRICING AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK, WITHOUT REGARD TO PRINCIPLES OF CONFLICTS OF LAW TO THE EXTENT THAT SUCH PRINCIPLES WOULD REQUIRE OR PERMIT THE APPLICATION OF LAWS OF ANOTHER JURISDICTION.
16. Consent to Jurisdiction. The Company agrees that any legal suit, action or proceeding against the Company brought by the Remarketing Agents or by any person, if any, who controls the Remarketing Agents within the meaning of Section 15 of the Act or Section 20 of the Exchange Act, arising out of or based upon this Remarketing Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby may be instituted in courts of its choosing, and submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of such courts in any suit, action or proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific venue (any state or Federal court in the Borough of Manhattan, The City of New York, New York) where the legal suit can be instituted, it gives more jurisdiction to the Company.  By making it courts of its choosing, and also saying that it submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of such courts, the document has been changed in its entirety. The original text waives 'any objection which it may now or hereafter have to the laying of venue of any such proceeding, and irrevocably submits to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of such courts in any suit, action or proceeding.'
perturbation.law_explanation: This section allows for transfer of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice. By mandating exclusive jurisdiction in courts of the company's choosing, it can potentially create inconvenience for the other parties, making the case more difficult to pursue.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts legal principles related to venue selection and fairness. The original text specifies venue in 'any state or Federal court in the Borough of Manhattan, The City of New York, New York,' waiving objections to venue. The changed text allows the Company to choose the courts, submitting to 'exclusive jurisdiction of such courts.' This creates an imbalance, potentially causing inconvenience and difficulty for the other parties, as noted in the Law Explanation. 28 U.S. Code § 1404 addresses 'Change of venue' for the 'convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice,' suggesting the original clause promotes fairness, but the changed clause undermines this principle by granting unilateral control of venue to the Company. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction, where a consumer protection is omitted.

perturbation.original_text: 21. Waiver of Jury Trial. The Company and each of the Remarketing Agents hereby irrevocably waives, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, any and all right to trial by jury in any legal proceeding arising out of or relating to this Remarketing Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby.
perturbation.changed_text: 21. Waiver of Jury Trial. The Company hereby waives any and all right to trial by jury in any legal proceeding arising out of or relating to this Remarketing Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mentions that the Company and each of the Remarketing Agents hereby waives the right to trial by jury. By removing 'each of the Remarketing Agents hereby', now the Remarketing Agents are free to have a trial by jury, thus causing a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Seventh Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases where the value in controversy exceeds $20.  While this right can be waived, the original agreement provided a mutual waiver. The revised text omits the Remarketing Agents' waiver, potentially exposing the Company to jury trials, which contradicts the original intent of the agreement to avoid jury trials for both parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that both the Company and the Remarketing Agents waive their right to a jury trial. The changed text removes the waiver for the Remarketing Agents, which contradicts the original agreement where both parties agreed to waive this right. The Seventh Amendment, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, preserves the right to trial by jury, 'absent waiver by the parties'. By omitting the Remarketing Agents' waiver, the changed text creates a situation where they can now potentially demand a jury trial, which goes against the original intention of mutual waiver. This omission constitutes a legal loophole, potentially harming the Company, aligning with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition.
